chunk_summary: This section discusses pediatric enteral and parenteral nutrition in India, emphasizing the impact of undernutrition, resource constraints, and the need for standardized protocols to improve outcomes in low-resource settings.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical importance of enteral and parenteral nutrition in pediatric care, especially in the Indian context.
Identify key challenges in implementing nutrition support in resource-limited settings.
Learn strategies to mitigate preventable harms such as aspiration, electrolyte shifts, and central line infections.
Recognize the role of standardized protocols in improving nutrition delivery and patient outcomes.
key_facts: NFHS-5 (2019–2021) reports 35.5% stunting and 19.3% wasting among under-5 children in India.
Enteral and parenteral nutrition directly influence wound healing, immune function, ventilator weaning, and hospital length of stay.
Key preventable harms in Indian wards include aspiration during tube feeds, electrolyte shifts (e.g., phosphate and magnesium during refeeding), and central line infections or thrombosis during PN.
Resource constraints in India include inconsistent availability of pumps, pH strips, commercial formulas, and refrigeration for feeds.
Protocols should define minimum safe standards such as hang times, flushing, delivered-volume documentation, and 'stop feeds' triggers.
reference_quote: National survey data from NFHS-5 (2019–2021) report stunting 35.5% and wasting 19.3% among under-5 children, so many hospitalized children start illness with limited reserves.
